title: Working Time Regulations 1998
desc: Implements the EU Working Time Directive (2003/88/EC) in Great Britain. Sets limits on weekly working hours, provides for rest breaks and rest periods, and establishes minimum annual leave entitlements. Significantly amended post-Brexit and by the Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023. The 2023 amendments reformed holiday pay calculation for irregular hours and part-year workers following Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC 21.

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.summary: Key definitions including worker, working time, rest period, night time, night worker, and shift worker.
refs: Tyco Integrated Security SL v Bankia [2015] (Travel as Working Time — CJEU)

parts.title: Rights and Obligations Concerning Working Time

sections.title: Maximum weekly working time
sections.summary: A worker's working time, including overtime, must not exceed an average of 48 hours per 7-day period, calculated over a 17-week reference period.
sections.text:
(1) Unless his employer has first obtained the worker's agreement in writing to perform such work, a worker's working time, including overtime, in any reference period applicable in his case shall not exceed an average of 48 hours for each 7 days of the period.

(2) An employer shall take all reasonable steps, in keeping with the need to protect the health and safety of workers, to ensure that the limit specified in paragraph (1) is complied with in the case of each worker employed by him in relation to whom it applies, and shall keep up-to-date records of all workers who carry out work to which that limit does not apply by reason of the fact that the employer has obtained the worker's agreement.

(3) Subject to paragraphs (4) and (5), the reference period applicable in the case of a worker is the period of 17 weeks.

sections.title: The 48-hour week — individual opt-out
sections.summary: A worker may agree in writing to work more than 48 hours per week. The opt-out can be terminated by the worker on minimum 7 days' notice (up to 3 months if agreed).
sections.text:
(1) The limit specified in regulation 4(1) shall not apply in relation to a worker who has agreed with his employer in writing that it should not apply in his case.

(2) An employer shall maintain up-to-date records of all workers who have entered into agreements referred to in paragraph (1).

(3) A worker may terminate an agreement referred to in paragraph (1) by giving to the employer notice of not less than—
(a) seven days (if there is no provision in the agreement for the worker to terminate it); or
(b) three months (if the agreement provides for a notice period of more than three months); or
(c) the notice period provided for in the agreement (in any other case).

(4) A worker who terminates an agreement under paragraph (3) is not entitled to be treated less favourably in accordance with these Regulations.
refs: Reg.31 WTR 1998 (Prohibition on Less Favourable Treatment)

sections.title: Length of night work
sections.summary: Night workers' normal hours of work must not exceed an average of 8 hours per 24-hour period. No averaging where special hazards or heavy mental or physical strain involved.
sections.text:
(1) A night worker's normal hours of work in any reference period applicable in his case shall not exceed an average of eight hours for each 24 hours of that period.

(3) The normal hours of work of a night worker shall not exceed eight hours in any 24-hour period during which the night worker works at night if those normal hours include special hazards or heavy physical or mental strain.

(7) An employer shall ensure that each night worker receives a free health assessment before being assigned to night work, and is offered a free health assessment at regular intervals while assigned to night work.
refs: Reg.7 WTR 1998 (Health Assessment for Night Workers)
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Reg.16 (Pregnant Night Workers)

sections.title: Daily rest
sections.summary: Adult workers are entitled to a minimum rest period of 11 consecutive hours in each 24-hour period during which they work.
sections.text:
(1) An adult worker is entitled to a rest period of not less than eleven consecutive hours in each 24-hour period during which he works for his employer.

(2) Subject to paragraph (3), a young worker is entitled to a rest period of not less than twelve consecutive hours in each 24-hour period during which he works for his employer.

sections.title: Weekly rest period
sections.summary: Adult workers are entitled to an uninterrupted rest period of not less than 24 hours per 7-day period, or 48 hours per 14-day period.
sections.text:
(1) Subject to paragraph (8), an adult worker is entitled to an uninterrupted rest period of not less than 24 hours in each seven-day period during which he works for his employer.

(2) Subject to paragraph (8), an adult worker is entitled to either—
(a) two uninterrupted rest periods each of not less than 24 hours in each 14-day period during which he works for his employer; or
(b) one uninterrupted rest period of not less than 48 hours in each such 14-day period.

sections.title: Rest breaks — in-work
sections.summary: Adult workers whose daily working time exceeds 6 hours are entitled to a rest break of at least 20 consecutive minutes, away from their workstation.
sections.text:
(1) Where an adult worker's daily working time is more than six hours, he is entitled to a rest break.

(2) The characteristics of the rest break to which a worker is entitled under this regulation are that—
(a) it is an uninterrupted period of not less than 20 minutes;
(b) the worker is entitled to spend it away from his workstation if he has one.
refs: Hughes v The Corps of Commissionaires [2011] (Rest Break Timing)

sections.title: Entitlement to annual leave
sections.summary: Workers are entitled to 4 weeks' paid annual leave per year under Reg.13. Reg.13A provides a further 1.6 weeks. Together: 5.6 weeks (28 days for a 5-day week worker).
sections.text:
(1) Subject to paragraph (5), a worker is entitled to 4 weeks' annual leave in each leave year.

(2) The periods of leave to which a worker is entitled under this regulation—
(a) may be taken in instalments,
(b) may not be replaced by a payment in lieu, except where the worker's employment is terminated.
refs: Reg.15 WTR 1998 (Dates on Which Leave May be Taken)

sections.title: Additional entitlement to annual leave
sections.summary: Workers are entitled to an additional 1.6 weeks' annual leave per year. Together with Reg.13: 5.6 weeks total (28 days for full-time workers).
sections.text:
(1) Subject to paragraph (5), a worker is entitled to a period of additional leave determined in accordance with this regulation in each leave year.

(2) The period of additional leave to which a worker is entitled under this regulation in any leave year is 1.6 weeks.

(3) The periods of leave to which a worker is entitled under this regulation—
(a) may be taken in instalments,
(b) may not be replaced by a payment in lieu, except where the worker's employment is terminated.

sections.title: Compensation related to entitlement to leave
sections.summary: On termination, a worker is entitled to payment in lieu of any leave entitlement accrued but not taken in the relevant leave year.
sections.text:
(1) Where a worker's employment is terminated during a leave year and on the date of termination the proportion of leave taken by the worker is less than the proportion of the year which has expired, his employer shall make him a payment in lieu of leave.

(3) The amount of any payment under paragraph (1) is A × (B − C) where A is the worker's remuneration for a day's leave; B is the amount of leave (in days) to which the worker is entitled in the leave year; C is the amount of leave (in days) taken by the worker in the leave year.
refs: Hartley v King Edward VI College [2017] (Holiday Pay on Termination)

sections.title: Dates on which leave is taken
sections.summary: Worker may choose when to take leave but must give employer twice as much notice as the length of the proposed leave. Employer may refuse or require leave on specified dates on notice.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may take leave to which he is entitled under regulation 13 or regulation 13A on such days as he may elect by giving notice to his employer in accordance with paragraph (3), subject to any requirement imposed under paragraph (2).

(3) A worker gives notice for the purposes of paragraph (1) by giving notice to his employer of the days on which leave is to be taken, not less than twice as many days in advance as the number of days or part-days to which the notice relates.

sections.title: Pay during annual leave
sections.summary: Workers are entitled to normal remuneration during annual leave. The Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023 reformed holiday pay calculation for irregular hours and part-year workers.
sections.text:
(1) A worker is entitled to be paid in respect of any period of annual leave to which he is entitled under regulation 13, at the rate of a week's pay in respect of each week of leave.

(2) Sections 221 to 224 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 shall apply for the purpose of determining the amount of a week's pay for the purposes of this regulation.

(3) The modifications are that the calculation date is the first day of the period of leave in question, and the references to the average number of weekly hours shall be taken as references to the average number of weekly hours in the period of 52 weeks ending with the last complete week before the calculation date.
sections.eraNote: Holiday pay for irregular hours workers (including zero hours workers and part-year workers) was reformed by the Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023, following Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC 21. From 1 January 2024, holiday entitlement for irregular hours and part-year workers accrues at 12.07% of hours worked in a pay period. This is one of the most significant WTR changes since 1998 and generates high ACEI risk for employers with variable hours workforces who have not updated their holiday pay calculations.
refs: ACAS Holiday Entitlement and Pay Guidance
Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton [2015] (Overtime and Holiday Pay)

sections.title: Unmeasured working time
sections.summary: Where a worker's working time is not measured or predetermined and the worker can determine the duration of their working time, the 48-hour week and night work limits may not apply.
sections.text:
(1) Regulations 4(1) and (2) and 6(1), (2) and (7) do not apply in relation to a worker where, on account of the specific characteristics of the activity in which he is engaged, the duration of his working time is not measured or predetermined or can be determined by the worker himself, and more particularly where such a worker is—
(a) a managing executive or other person with autonomous decision-taking powers;
(b) a family worker; or
(c) a worker officiating at religious ceremonies in churches and religious communities.

sections.title: Other exceptional cases
sections.summary: Exceptions to daily rest, weekly rest, and rest break provisions for activities involving continuity of service, foreseeable surges, and shift changes.

sections.title: Compensatory rest
sections.summary: Where workers are excepted from daily or weekly rest periods, the employer must give compensatory rest as soon as reasonably practicable.
sections.text: (1) Where the application of any provision of these Regulations is excluded by regulation 21 or 22, or is modified by a relevant agreement under regulation 23, and a worker is accordingly required by his employer to work during a period which would otherwise be a rest period or rest break, his employer shall wherever possible allow him to take an equivalent period of compensatory rest, and in exceptional cases in which it is not possible, for objective reasons, to grant such a period of rest, his employer shall afford him such protection as may be appropriate in order to safeguard his health and safety.

sections.title: Enforcement — individual entitlements
sections.summary: A worker may present a complaint to the employment tribunal that an employer has refused to allow them to exercise a right under the WTR. Three-month time limit.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that his employer has refused to permit him to exercise any right he has under—
(i) regulation 10(1) or (2) (daily rest),
(ii) regulation 11(1), (2) or (3) (weekly rest),
(iii) regulation 12(1) (rest breaks),
(iv) regulation 13 (annual leave), or
(v) regulation 13A (additional annual leave);
or has failed to pay him the whole or any part of any amount due to him under regulation 14(2) or 16(1).

(2) An employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this regulation unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date on which it is alleged that the exercise of the right should have been permitted.

sections.title: Remedies
sections.summary: Where a tribunal finds a complaint well-founded it shall make a declaration and may award compensation. Claims for underpaid holiday pay may go back up to 2 years.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employment tribunal finds a complaint under regulation 28 well-founded, the tribunal—
(a) shall make a declaration to that effect, and
(b) may award the worker compensation.

(2) The amount of any compensation awarded under paragraph (1)(b) shall be such as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances.

(3) An employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint presented under regulation 28(1)(b) where the deduction was made more than two years before the date of presentation of the complaint, unless the complaint is presented during the period of six months beginning with the date on which the worker ceases to be employed by the employer.
refs: Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton [2015] (Overtime and Holiday Pay)

sections.title: Right not to suffer detriment
sections.summary: Workers have the right not to suffer detriment or be dismissed for exercising or seeking to exercise their rights under the WTR.
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker—
(a) refused (or proposed to refuse) to comply with a requirement which the employer imposed in contravention of these Regulations,
(b) refused (or proposed to refuse) to forgo a right conferred on him by these Regulations,
(c) failed to sign a workforce agreement for the purposes of these Regulations,
(d) performed (or proposed to perform) any functions as a representative or candidate,
(e) brought proceedings against the employer to enforce a right conferred on him by these Regulations, or
(f) alleged that the employer had infringed such a right.
refs: s.101A ERA 1996 (Automatic Unfair Dismissal for WTR)

parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The WTR 1998 were made under the European Communities Act 1972 to implement the Working Time Directive. As a negative resolution SI, they were laid before Parliament and came into force without requiring affirmative vote. The UK initially challenged the legal base of the Directive (Case C-84/94 UK v Council [1996]) but lost before the ECJ. The opt-out from the 48-hour week (Reg.5) was a UK-specific derogation negotiated by the Major government.

precedingDocuments.title: Working Time Directive 93/104/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: The original EU Directive that the WTR 1998 implemented. Established the 48-hour week, rest periods, and 4 weeks' annual leave.

precedingDocuments.title: UK v Council (Case C-84/94) [1996] ECR I-5755
precedingDocuments.significance: The UK challenged the Working Time Directive's legal base (health and safety vs social policy). The ECJ upheld the Directive as a valid health and safety measure under Art.118a EEC Treaty. The UK was required to implement it.

precedingDocuments.title: Working Time Directive 2003/88/EC (Codified)
precedingDocuments.significance: Codified version of the Working Time Directive incorporating all amendments. References to '2003/88/EC' and '93/104/EC' refer to the same substantive obligations.

precedingDocuments.title: Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023 (SI 2023/1325)
precedingDocuments.publisher: Secretary of State for Business and Trade
precedingDocuments.significance: Post-Brexit reform of holiday pay calculation for irregular hours and part-year workers. Introduced the 12.07% accrual method from 1 January 2024, responding to Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC 21.

leadingCases.name: Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC 21
leadingCases.heldText: Part-year workers are entitled to 5.6 weeks' holiday per year, not a pro-rated amount. The Supreme Court rejected the 12.07% calculation for permanent part-year workers under the pre-2023 rules.
leadingCases.significance: Triggered the Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023 which introduced the 12.07% method for irregular/part-year workers from Jan 2024.

leadingCases.name: Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton [2015] ICR 221
leadingCases.heldText: Non-guaranteed overtime that is normally worked must be included in holiday pay calculations for the first 4 weeks of leave (Reg.13 leave derived from the Directive).
leadingCases.significance: Extended holiday pay to include regularly worked overtime. Massive financial exposure for employers.

leadingCases.name: Lock v British Gas Trading Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 315
leadingCases.heldText: Commission payments must be included in holiday pay where they form part of normal remuneration.
leadingCases.significance: Extended holiday pay to include commission — affects sales-based workforces.

leadingCases.name: Tyco Integrated Security SL v Bankia (Case C-266/14) [2015]
leadingCases.court: Court of Justice of the EU
leadingCases.heldText: Travel time from home to the first client and from the last client to home constitutes working time for mobile workers who have no fixed workplace.
leadingCases.significance: Extended the definition of working time to include travel for mobile workers.

leadingCases.name: Williams v British Airways plc [2011] UKSC 51
leadingCases.heldText: Holiday pay must reflect 'normal remuneration' — supplements and allowances intrinsically linked to the performance of tasks must be included.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'normal remuneration' principle for holiday pay calculation.

pendingAmendments: Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2023 — 12.07% accrual method in effect from Jan 2024
ERA 2025 — Fair Work Agency enforcement of holiday pay (not yet in force)
Post-Brexit retained EU law status — WTR now assimilated law under REUL Act 2023
